dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for unconscious patient at Salvation Army.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I came in and suddenly felt really dizzy. I fell forward, but I don't think I passed out.
I saw him when he was just sitting on the chair. He looked really out of breath and had trouble with his leg.
Hi sir, can you tell me your name?
Robert... and my birthday is May 3rd.
Good. Can you tell me which county we're in?
Richmond County.
What's today's date?
I think it's the 18th, maybe the 19th.
Tell me what happened when you came into Salvation Army.
I walked in and suddenly felt dizzy, then fell forward onto the floor.
Responsive clear to person, place, time, and situation. Let me check your breathing.
Can we do a quick exam on your chest? We'll listen to your lungs.
Sir, can we take your oxygen saturation now?
Oxygen saturation is 93% on room air.
Are you wheezing or having trouble breathing?
I feel like it's tight on the right side.
You're wheezing on the right, mostly on exhalation. Let me grab some oxygen.
We are going to provide some oxygen via nasal cannula at 6 liters per minute.
Respirations are 27 per minute, unlabored despite wheezing on the right.
Pulse is 90 beats per minute, regular rate and rhythm, normal quality.
Are you experiencing any chest pain?
No chest pain, just shortness of breath.
Partner, I'm going to get a blood pressure reading now.
Blood pressure is 127 over 65, even and strong pulse.
Robert, you mentioned your knee hurt after the fall. Any other pain or discomfort?
Just the dizziness and the tightness in my chest. My knee really hurts, though.
What were you doing before you walked in and felt dizzy?
I had a small snack, something with juice and water. No full meal in the last hour prior to feeling dizzy.
From what he had just told me, he has a history of hypertension and COPD. He mentioned that before.
Let me attach the 4-lead ECG monitor before continuing with our treatment. You'll need to hold still for just a moment, please.
4-lead EKG shows a sinus rhythm with occasional junctional rhythms and occasional premature ventricular contractions.
Are your eyes reactive and focused on my voice and hands as I move them up and down in front of you?
Still wheezing on the right side particularly on expiration. Breath sounds are mostly equal otherwise.

I think you might be having some wheezing due to your COPD. We are going to give you a nebulizer treatment using a combination of Albuterol and Atrovent, as per protocol for respiratory distress associated with COPD.
Nebulizer is set and ready with Albuterol 5 mg and Atrovent 0.5 mg. Take a few deep breaths through the mouthpiece, and it should help relax your airways and ease your breathing.
Oxygen saturation has improved to 98% after the nebulizer treatment.
You should get some relief with that. How do you feel now compared to before the nebulizer treatment initiated?
Feels better. I can breathe easier. Still almost tight on the right, but definitely more manageable than prior.
Breathing at 27 per minute, still above normal range but showing improvement with the treatment and oxygen administration in place.
Blood pressure is 127/65 now-same as initial, indicating stability in cardiovascular status.
Any more trouble with your breathing now, Robert? Are the wheezing and tightness still present or worsening?
Still breathing through the tightness. No chest pain, thank goodness, and no other pain reported at this time either.

We are following the Respiratory Distress Protocol per guidelines for patients presenting with respiratory wheezing, shortness of breath, and a known medical history consistent with COPD and hypertension.

I've completed the 12-lead EKG, which shows a mild abnormality in the inferior and posterior leads, suggesting possible acute posterior infarction with some extension to inferior territory. The rhythm is currently still in sinus, but with some irregular elements that were previously noted on the 4-lead. I've documented this as a possible STEMI for hospital communication during handoff.

It's best we stay with the initial protocol since the improvement is not complete. We'll reassess once stabilized or after any visible clinical changes. No chest pain reported, but the abnormal EKG may be worthy of continued monitoring as well as perpendicular to breathing distress assessed earlier.
We're going to help you up to the stretcher, okay? Just walk slowly and try to stay steady until we're on the stretcher for you.
Robert, do you feel like you can get up with assistance? Your knee seems to be an issue - any pain associated with movement now, prior to the transport phase starting?
Yeah, I can walk with help. My left knee isn't steady, but I was able to walk from the chair unaided earlier despite the pain from the fall at the entrance.
What hospital did you want to go to besides for the need to address the breathing issue from the wheezing and dizziness? Any specific ER preference?
I just want to get my knee checked out. St. Mary's please. That's where I usually go for ER appointments and familiar staff.
Great. We're heading to St. Mary's then. Let me cool down the stretcher, and I'll assist with the lift - two-person lift, so as to minimize strain on your injured knee.
All notes are ready, medications and vitals are recorded. I've reviewed and double-checked the ePCR for documentation continuity. Let's get you to the hospital.

The patient being placed on the stretcher is steady enough for transport. No pain reported during the process per EPCR note: pain-0. Physical state remains conscious and oriented as previously assessed by responsible EMT team.
I've flagged St. Mary's. Paramedics will be ready on arrival. Please make sure the patient is comfortable and monitored continuously during transport.

Below were all the medications and treatments we provided. Let the ED know about the nebulizer therapy with Albuterol and Atrovent as part of the respiratory distress treatment in addition to administered high-flow oxygen post-treatment intervention.
You're safe now. Let's get you there. Oxygen will continue during the ride as EPCR specifies O2 at 6LPM via Nasal Cannula during transport and prior to hospital transfer.
We're heading to St. Mary's in auto mode. Let's get you set for a smooth ride with no interruptions.
Oxygen supply is maintained during transport as recommended in ePCR: O2 was administered per protocol at 6LPM via nasal cannula.
Transportation complete. Patient safe and awake upon arrival. No pain reported at time of transport per recorded data (pain-0). Patient is in stable clinical condition during transit.
He says he's ready for the hospital now. Any questions or concerns before transfer?
Looks good. Thank you for the thorough care. All pre-hospital data and treatment administered are correctly provided and understood. Will manage from here.
All done here. Cleaning up and preparing for the next call. Will log out of the transport phase once vehicle arrives at destination and patient is turned over to facility.
All protocol actions have been followed. Patient is stable and satisfactorily transported. No missed EPCRs or violations of mandated on-field procedures based on documented notes.
Handing off now for triage at St. Mary's ER as per patient request upon clinical readiness as assessed by EMS team.
We've taken a glucose assessment earlier but it returned a flatline result, indicating inability to obtain a reading per the ePCR.
